coredata.dc:title: Enhanced selective detection of Sudan III dye in cosmetics utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) combined with Molecularly Imprinted Polymer (MIP)

coredata.dc:description: Sudan III, a commonly used dye in cosmetics, poses health risks due to its potential carcinogenicity and allergenic properties, necessitating rigorous monitoring of its illegal presence in consumer goods. In this study, a novel fluorometric assay employing Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots coated with Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (NCQDs/SiO2@MIP) is presented to accurately and sensitively detect Sudan III. Unlike other majority analytes, Sudan III shows fluorescence enhancement upon binding with our NCQDs-MIPs. Under optimal conditions—pH 7.0, histidine buffer, 15 min incubation, and 500 mg/L NCQDs/SiO2@MIP—the method achieves a low 1.4 nM (0.49 ppb) detection limit and a broad linear range (5–800 nM). With precision (RSD < 5 %) and selectivity surpassing Sudan dyes and common cosmetic ingredients, the method’s reliability is evident. Cross-validation against LC-MS/MS confirms the accuracy of Sudan III quantification in real cosmetic samples. This NCQDs/SiO2@MIP fluorometric sensor holds promise for rapid on-site detection of Sudan III and other contaminants in cosmetics, offering superior sensitivity, selectivity, and minimal sample preparation requirements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra of extraction mixtures were recorded after each wash. (B) Representative fluorescence spectra of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP were measured after each wash. (C) Comparison the fluorescense spectra of NCQDs/SiO2@NIP, NCQDs/SiO2@MIP only and after adding Sudan III. (D) The proposed mechanism of Sudan III detection using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP.

Characterization of prepared NCQDs, NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. TEM image of (A) NCQDs and (B) NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. SEM image of (C) NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and (D) NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. (E) XRD pattern of NCQDs. The EDS results of (F) NCQDs and (G) NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. (H) FTIR data of the prepared composites including CQDs, NCQDs, NCQDs/SiO2@NIP, and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. XPS spectra of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP: (I) Full-scale XPS spectra of the CQDs; (J) high-resolution of C 1s spectrum; (K) high-resolution of N 1s spectrum; (L) high-resolution of O 1s spectrum; and (M) high-resolution of Si 2p spectrum.

Optimization conditions for Sudan III detection using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. Effect of (A) pH, (B) Buffer composition and concentrations, (C) incubation time, and (D) NCQDs/SiO2@MIP amount on the enhancement of fluorescence intensities of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP in the presence of Sudan III (450 nM). Error bars show the standard deviations of three replicates.

Quantititative analysis of Sudan III using synthesize NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. (A)The representative fluorescence spectra of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP in the presences of different concentrations of Sudan III. (B) The calibration curve was constructed for Sudan III determination under the optimal conditions using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and NCQDs/SiO2@NIP.

The selectivity analysis for Sudan III detection of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP sensor. (A) Fluorescence intensity comparison between NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and NCQDs/SiO2@NIP in the presence of various Sudan dyes, with Sudan III at 0.25 µM and other Sudan dyes (Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan IV, Sudan 7B) each at 1.25 µM. (B) Influence of 1.25 µM concentrations of Sudan dyes (Sudan I, Sudan II, Sudan IV, and Sudan 7B) on the fluorescence response of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP to 0.25 µM Sudan III. (C) The fluoresnce intensity of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP in the presence of various substances (a: Phenoxyethanol; b: Sodium Benzoate; c: Laura acid; d: Vitamin E; e: Mg2+; f: Zn2+; g: Cu2+; h: Ag+; i: Amarath dye; k: Sunset dye; l: Ponceau 4R) at 250 µM. (D) The effect of 11 substances on the fluorescence respons of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP to 0.25 µM of Sudan III. Error bars show the standard deviations of three replicates.

head.ce:title: Enhanced selective detection of Sudan III dye in cosmetics utilizing nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) combined with Molecularly Imprinted Polymer (MIP)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sudan III, a commonly used dye in cosmetics, poses health risks due to its potential carcinogenicity and allergenic properties, necessitating rigorous monitoring of its illegal presence in consumer goods. In this study, a novel fluorometric assay employing Nitrogen-doped Carbon Quantum Dots coated with Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (NCQDs/SiO2@MIP) is presented to accurately and sensitively detect Sudan III. Unlike other majority analytes, Sudan III shows fluorescence enhancement upon binding with our NCQDs-MIPs. Under optimal conditions—pH 7.0, histidine buffer, 15 min incubation, and 500 mg/L NCQDs/SiO2@MIP—the method achieves a low 1.4 nM (0.49 ppb) detection limit and a broad linear range (5–800 nM). With precision (RSD < 5 %) and selectivity surpassing Sudan dyes and common cosmetic ingredients, the method’s reliability is evident. Cross-validation against LC-MS/MS confirms the accuracy of Sudan III quantification in real cosmetic samples. This NCQDs/SiO2@MIP fluorometric sensor holds promise for rapid on-site detection of Sudan III and other contaminants in cosmetics, offering superior sensitivity, selectivity, and minimal sample preparation requirements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The significance of color in cosmetic product marketing underscores the need for a meticulouss examination of synthetic dyes, the predominant coloring agents in the industry [1,2]. The imperative for analytical control arises from potential health implications and varying regulatory standards globally. Azo dyes, known for their cost-effectiveness and derived from aromatic amines, raise concerns due to studies revealing mutagenic, genotoxic, and carcinogenic effects linked to their chromophoric azo group [3,4].

Numerous nations have implemented bans on azo dyes such as Sudan dyes in food items, along with stringent regulatory measures concerning cosmetics. Specifically, Sudan III, referred to as D&C Red No. 17, is sanctioned as a coloring agent in externally applied medications and cosmetics within the United States [5]. Its usage is similarly approved in cosmetics across the European Union, Japan, and various other nations. However, it's important to note that Sudan III's application is restricted to coloring externally applied cosmetics and is strictly prohibited in products intended for use around the eyes or on the lips [5]. Despite these regulations, Sudan III is still legally used in some cosmetic products. The illicit use of Sudan III persists in products intended for use in the eye area or on the lips, such as lipstick, particularly in developing nations. This highlights the urgency of developing rapid and sensitive methods for determining and monitoring the use of Sudan III in various cosmetic products.

Current detection techniques, such as near-infrared (NIR) or Raman spectroscopy [6], electrochemical detection [7], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9] and colorimetric detection [10,11], face challenges with costly equipment, intricate sample preparation, prolonged analysis, and complex setups, rendering them unsuitable for routine use. Analyzing Sudan III in real-life samples presents challenges due to its low concentration and interference from other substances in the sample matrix. This highlights the urgent need for an efficient, highly selective, and environmentally friendly technique for its identification in cosmetic samples.

Fluorescent sensors are attractive due to their notable characteristics such as affordability, ease of use, quick analysis, and high sensitivity. They have become the focus of attention for developing quantitative analytical techniques by utilizing changes in fluorescent intensity or emission peak position, either through enhancement or quenching [12–14]. Unlike other semiconductor quantum dots, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) present straightforward synthesis processes, efficient dispersal in water, remarkable photostability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and enhanced chemical stability [14–16]. While traditional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess advantageous features, they suffer from limitations in fluorescence yield [17,18]. To address this challenge, doping CQDs with heteroatoms has been shown to efficiently enhance their optical properties [19,20]. Among various heteroatoms, nitrogen dopants have gained significant attention due to their broad precursor range, natural abundance in sustainable sources, and cost-effective synthesis methods [21]. Moreover, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) exhibit higher sensitivity than undoped CQDs due to their high quantum yield [22–24]. Nitrogen dopants offer a suitable atomic size for incorporation into CQD structures, strong electronegativity for interaction with carbon, and an accessible lone pair of electrons for tuning electronic properties [25]. NCQDs find applications in catalysis [26], biological imaging [27], and fluorescent sensing [24,28] due to their high quantum yield. Their ability to control optoelectronic properties through various synthetic methods distinguishes them from undoped CQDs [29].

However, the benefits of CQDs and NCQDs have been tempered by reduced selectivity in their use as probes, linked to fluorescence enhancement [30,31] or quenching [14,32,33] in the presence of specific substances or sample constituents. To address this, surface modification techniques involving target-specific materials, such as Molecularly Imprinted Polymers (MIPs), have been explored to enhance probe selectivity [34]. MIPs, renowned for their high stability and less stringent reaction conditions, offer enhanced selectivity for targeted molecules [35]. MIPs are synthesized through the copolymerization of functional monomers and cross-linkers in the presence of a template molecule. The combination of MIPs with CQDs has attracted interest because of the high sensitivity of CQDs and the selectivity of MIPs [34,36].

The application of silica in coating MIPs onto CQDs has gained considerable interest in recent years. This interest stems from silica's highly cross-linked rigid matrix, which is adept at creating recognition sites while maintaining the optical properties of CQDs [34,37]. Among the array of methods for preparing CQDs coated with MIPs, the Stöber method has emerged as a preferred approach due to its simplicity and cost-effectiveness. This method finds widespread use in crafting various nanomaterials and modifying polymer surfaces. Importantly, MIPs synthesized via the Stöber method feature selective cavities with extended lifetimes, owing to the robust and stable structures of silica-based materials, which exhibit great thermal and mechanical stability. Additionally, the Stöber method facilitates the efficient removal of the template from the polymer, further enhancing its efficacy in MIP synthesis [38].

Numerous studies have explored the combined potential of MIPs and CQDs, capitalizing on MIPs' ability to selectively recognize alongside the sensitivity of fluorescence detection [39]. For instance, the Stöber method was used to prepare MIPs-coated CDs, demonstrating selectively identify oxytetracycline [40]. Additionally, a novel fluorescence sensor utilizing MIP-coated carbon quantum dots (CQDs@SiO2@MIPs) was developed for the selective and sensitive determination of Sudan I in chili powder and ketchup [41]. However, despite the promising capabilities of these materials, their applications for the analysis of other Sudan dyes, such as Sudan III, remain unexplored.

This study presents a simple, eco-friendly, selective, and sensitive fluorescence assay for detecting Sudan III in cosmetics. Unlike other studies that rely on analyte detection through the quenching effect, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer [14,41,42] or inner filter effect [43], our approach showcases a significant enhancement in the fluorescence of NCQDs/MIP upon the presence of Sudan III. Notably, the sensor exhibits remarkable selectivity in detecting Sudan III, even amidst the presence of other structurally similar Sudan dyes, underscoring its high specificity.
a. Materials and apparatus

Most reagents utilized in this study were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. These included citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (CH4N2O), 3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), Tetraethyl Orthosilicate(TEOS), and 1-[(4-phenyldiazenylphenyl)diazenyl]naphthalen-2-ol (C22H16N4O). Sudan dyes (Sudan I, II, IV, 7B, and Sudan III) were obtained from Alfa Aesar.

Solvents used encompassed ethanol (99.5 %), n-hexane (95 %), acetone, and buffer solutions consisting of histidine, Tris, and phosphate buffer from Acros Organics. Additionally, preservatives and additives such as phenoxyethanol, sodium benzoate, lauric acid, and vitamin E were employed. Various dyes, including amaranth dye, sunset dye, and Ponceau 4R, were also sourced from Sigma-Aldrich, USA.

Fluorescence analysis was performed using a F-4700 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi) with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and an emission spectral range selected between 380 – 600 nm. Meanwhile UV–Vis measuments were conducted with a UV-1601 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The analysis of morphology, and chemical and crystal structures for NCQDs and CQDs was carried out employing FTIR-1S Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (Shimadzu), JEM 1010 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL), JSM-IT200 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM, JEOL) in conjunction with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), and X-ray Diffraction D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker) utilizing a Cu Kα exciting source at λ = 1.54 Å, respectively.
b. Synthesis of CQDs and NCQDs

NCQDs were prepared via thermal decomposition, using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source. In the specific procedure, a solid mixture containing 7.0 g of citric acid and 3.0 g of urea was thermally treated at 200 °C for 12 h, forming a dark brown solid powder. The finely ground mixture (0.20 g) was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and then exposed to ultrasonic vibration for 15 min. The resulting NCQDs solution was passed through a 0.65 μm membrane filter to eliminate large insoluble particles. The concentrated NCQDs solution achieved displayed a deep brown hue and was then stored at 4 °C for further procedures.

CQDs were synthesized using a similar procedure, except that 10.0 g of citric acid were directly heated, omitting the urea component in the NCQDs synthesis. The CQDs were prepared to compare quantum efficiency with the NCQDs.
c. Functionalization of NCQDs with APTES

A 5.0 mL solution of NCQDs (4.0 mg/mL), synthesized as described above, was prepared in 45 mL of ethanol. The mixture was stirred at 40 °C with a rotation speed of 1,500 rpm. Subsequently, 360 μL of APTES (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane was added to the solution, and stirring continued for 30 min. As a result of APTES hydrolysis, the NCQDs mixture exhibited the formation of brownish-yellow precipitates. The mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtration to remove the insoluble residue. The resulting NCQDs/SiO2 solution remained transparent and was stored at 4 °C for further use.
d. Synthesis of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and non-imprinted polymer-coated NCQDs (NCQDs/SiO2@NIP)

A mixture containing 20 mL of n-hexane, 5.0 mL of NCQDs/SiO2 solution, and 1.0 mL of Sudan III/ACN (1.0 mM) was stirred for 5 min. Subsequently, 360 μL of APTES (functional monomer), 1000 μL of TEOS (cross-linker), and 240 μL of ammonia (initiator, 25–28 %) were added to the solution, followed by continuous stirring at room temperature for 1 h. After this duration, the solution transformed into a gel-like state (polymer). An additional 10 mL of acetone was introduced to disrupt the emulsion.

The resulting mixture was centrifuged to collect the solid portion. To completely remove Sudan III (the template molecule) from the polymer, the solid was washed with an EtOH/ACN (8:2) elution solution. Subsequently, 2.0 mL of the elution solution was added to the solid polymer, thoroughly mixed, and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was discarded, and this washing process was repeated four times. Finally, the solid mixture was dried at 800 °C for 12 h, yielding the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP material.
The NCQDs/SiO2@NIP material was synthesized using a similar procedure but without the presence of Sudan III in the initial solution.
e. Fluorescence analysis of Sudan III by NCQDs/SiO2@MIP

To begin, 5.0 mg of the synthesized NCQDs/SiO2@MIP material was dissolved in 10.0 mL of a histidine buffer (0.01 M) at pH 7. Then, a specific volume of Sudan III stock solution was added to 2.7 mL of the above mixture. Subsequently, histidine buffer was introduced to reach a total volume of 3 mL. Following this, the mixtures were allowed to incubate for 15 min at room temperature (25 °C) before measurement. For acquiring fluorescence spectra, an excitation wavelength of 360 nm was utilized, and the emission spectral range was set between 380–600 nm. The maximum fluorescence intensity (F) was recorded at an emission wavelength (λem) of 445 nm.

The values of F0 and F were determined as follows: F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP solution in the absence of Sudan III, while F corresponds to the fluorescence intensity with varying concentrations of Sudan III. The difference between F and F0, indicated as F – F0, serves as the analytical signal for this method.
f. Sample preparation for Sudan III analysis in lipstick

In this study, lipstick samples, obtained from various e-commerce platforms without identifiable brands, were selected as the target samples. The procedure involved finely grinding and weighing 0.50 g of lipstick, which was then dissolved in 10 mL of n-hexane. After thorough shaking, the resulting mixture underwent ultrasonic vibration for 30 min. Following this, the solution underwent centrifugation at 15,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was then collected. A nitrogen gas purge was performed to enrich the sample further until the remaining solution volume reached approximately 0.5–1 mL. The pretreated sample was then quantitatively diluted with acetonitrile (ACN) to a final volume of 5.00 mL. The resulting post-dilution sample solution is suitable for subsequent analytical measurements.

In the synthesis process of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, Sudan III served as the template molecule. After the removal of the template, the cross-linked mesoporous polymer matrix retained binding sites tailored to the size, shape, and functionality of Sudan III, thereby facilitating the specific re-binding of the analyte. To assess the efficiency of Sudan III removal post-synthesis, we examined UV–vis absorption spectra of the extraction mixtures after each washing step, employing an ethanol:acetonitrile solvent system (8:2 v/v) (Fig. 1 A). Notably, the solution displayed a peak absorption at 510 nm, attributed to the presence of Sudan III dye. Following four washes, the absorbance of Sudan III in the eluent became negligible, as confirmed by the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Interestingly, the removal of Sudan III resulted in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs-MIP (Fig. 1B). Furthermore, upon binding Sudan III, the fluorescence intensity of prepared NCQDs-MIP was notably higher compared to both NCQDs-MIP and NCQDs-NIP (Fig. 1C), implying a potential enhancement effect of Sudan III on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs. The fluorescence enhancement observed with Sudan III cannot be solely attributed to the MIP coating, as the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs alone increases by approximately 20 % in the presence of 500 nM Sudan III (Fig. S1). This enhancement behavior may be due to the initial passivation of surface defects at low concentrations [44–46]. Trapping states at surface defects function as non-radiative recombination centers, and the competition between radiative and non-radiative pathways results in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of quantum dots [47,48]. By passivating the surface, the density of surface trap states can be reduced, which helps prevent non-radiative recombination and subsequently enhances the fluorescence intensity of the material [48]. Interestingly, the addition of high concentrations (up to 3000 nM) of Sudan III to the NCQDs solution resulted in significant fluorescence quenching (Fig. S1). and the saturation of surface traps on the QDs at high concentrations, which could destabilize the QDs and lead to quenching [49,50]. The fluorescence lifetimes of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP are extended in the presence of 500 nM of Sudan III and can be fitted with a single-exponential model (9.73 ns compared to 9.38 ns for NCQDs/SiO2@MIP alone; Fig. S2). This increase is likely due to the reduction of fast non-radiative decay components, indicating fewer surface defect traps. However, a more in-depth investigation into the underlying mechanisms, particularly the interaction with Sudan III, is required and lies beyond the scope of this study [51]. For this study, aimed at detecting trace levels of Sudan III in unauthorized cosmetic products intended for use around the eyes or on the lips, we focus on the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon for detecting Sudan III at low concentrations.

Drawing on the observed optical properties of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP with and without Sudan III, a mechanism for detecting Sudan III is proposed (Fig. 1D). APTES, TEOS, and Sudan III were utilized as a functional monomer, a cross-linker, and a template molecule, respectively, in the synthesis of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. Following the removal of Sudan III, binding sites complementary to Sudan III molecules were formed on the MIP layer. These recognition sites on NCQDs-MIP facilitate the specific binding of Sudan III, resulting in high selectivity and preventing binding to other analytes. Hence, the successful synthesis of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP sets the stage for further characterization and its application in Sudan III analysis.

The morphologies of NCQDs and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP were characterized using TEM and SEM techniques. The NCQDs appeared as spherical particles with a uniform size distribution, ranging mainly from 2 to 8 nm and an average diameter of 4 nm (Fig. 2 A). The NCQDs/SiO2@MIP exhibited a distinctive core–shell structure (Fig. 2B). During the synthesis of NCQDs/SiO2, the hydrolysis of APTES induced the aggregation of NCQDs nanoparticles, resulting in diameters ranging from 10 to 20 nm, which were subsequently stabilized with a thin layer of silica for further reactions. Following the hydrolysis of APTES, sol–gel polymerization was employed to fabricate NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. Surface morphology investigations revealed that both NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP materials exhibit a polymer shell encapsulating NCQDs with sizes ranging from 100 to 300 nm (Fig. 2C and D). These observed morphologies align with previous literature reports [52–54]. Notably, the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP material displays a more porous structure compared to NCQDs/SiO2@NIP. This enhanced porosity arises from the cavities forming after removing Sudan III, which demonstrate the presence of selective binding sites within MIP with Sudan III upon futher adsorption with Sudan III.

The XRD pattern of NCQDs displays a broad peak around 2θ = 20° (Fig. 2E), which corresponds to the (002) diffraction planes typical of carbon materials [55], indicating the graphitization of carbon atoms constituting the core of NCQDs. This data is consistent with previous findings [31,56,57] and exhibits no noticeable differences compared to the XRD pattern of CQDs [14,58].

The elemental composition of the samples was assessed through the EDS technique (Fig. 2F and 2G), yielding insightful data regarding their chemical makeup. For NCQDs, the nitrogen doping process was confirmed by the presence of carbon (52.65 %), oxygen (30.68 %), and nitrogen (16.67 %), indicative of successful nitrogen incorporation (Fig. 2F). Moreover, EDS analysis of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP revealed the formation of a thin MIP layer primarily composed of SiO2 (Fig. 2G). This was evidenced by notably high mass percentages of silicon and oxygen, affirming the effective incorporation of SiO2 into the composite structure. These findings underscore the successful synthesis and functionalization of NCQDs and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, providing crucial insights into their elemental compositions for further characterization and application in various fields.

The surface functional groups of CQDs, NCQDs, NCQDs/SiO2@NIP, and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP were scrutinized via FTIR analysis (Fig. 2H). In the CQDs sample, a broad characteristic peak around 3445 cm−1 was observed, attributed to the O–H stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups. Conversely, the NCQDs sample peaked at 3176 cm−1, indicating the N-H stretching vibration [59]. Additionally, the FTIR data of NCQDs displayed a peak at 1596 cm−1, assigned to C=N/N–H bending, along with peaks at 1344 and 1268 cm−1 attributed to C–N (aromatic amine) stretching, suggesting the successful doping of urea as nitrogen sources into the NCQDs. Furthermore, the surface groups of NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP were elucidated via FT-IR spectra. The significant peak observed at approximately 1035 cm−1 was attributed to the Si–O–Si stretching vibration, while the characteristic peak around 769 cm−1 indicated the Si–O–Si symmetrical stretching vibration. Additionally, a broad peak detected at approximately 447 cm−1 signified the Si–O symmetric bending vibration, thus confirming the presence of TEOS [60]. Additionally, bands at 1595 cm−1 represented the N–H bond of APTES, while the peak at 2940 cm−1 signified the C–H stretching vibration [61]. These observations collectively corroborated the successful application of APTES and TEOS in synthesizing CQDs@SiO2@MIPs.

To comprehensively characterize the surface properties and chemical composition of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, we employed X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The broad-scan XPS spectrum revealed the presence of oxygen (O 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), carbon (C 1s), and silicon (Si 2p) elements in the prepared NCQDs/SiO2@MIP (Fig. 2I). Deconvolution of the C (1s) spectrum yielded three primary binding peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to C–C/C=C, C–O/C–N, and C=O functionalities on the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP surface [62–64] (Fig. 2J). The narrow-scan N 1s XPS exhibited three predominant peaks at 399.2, 399.7, and 401.3 eV, indicative of pyridinic N, N–H, and amide C–N moieties, respectively [65] (Fig. 2K). Furthermore, the high-resolution O 1s spectrum revealed two binding peaks at 531.0 and 532.9 eV, corresponding to O=C and O–Si/O–C groups [66,67] (Fig. 2L). Notably, the oxygen content significantly influences the emission properties of N-CQDs by maintaining a balance between sp2 and sp3 carbon atoms [65,68]. Additionally, the Si–O group was confirmed by the high-resolution Si 2p spectrum, which displayed a peak at 103.5 eV [69] (Fig. 2M). Collectively, these findings validate the successful synthesis and functionalization of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP.

Fluorescence spectroscopy was utilized to characterize the optical properties of CQDs and NCQDs (Fig. S3). Both materials exhibited a maximum excitation wavelength at 360 nm, with strong fluorescence emission peaks observed at 440 nm upon excitation at this wavelength. Notably, NCQDs containing heterocyclic nitrogens demonstrated significantly higher fluorescence intensity than citric acid-based CQDs. The nitrogen doping led to a second peak at 330 nm in the absorption spectra, attributed to the increased energy levels, particularly from the non-bonding π orbital of nitrogen [70]. Quantum yield analysis, utilizing a slope method with Quinine in 0.5 M H2SO4 (QY = 54.6 % at 310 nm) as reference [71], revealed quantum yields of 20.8 % and 10.0 % for NCQDs and CQDs, respectively, under 360 nm excitation. Thus, the significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity observed in NCQDs offers the potential for increasing the sensitivity of Sudan III detection in the next step.

To evaluate the long-term fluorescence stability, we conducted continuous monitoring of the fluorescence intensity in the composite material comprising MIPs and NIPs over a 30-day period (Fig. S4). Both synthesized materials exhibited robust fluorescence stability. After this extended duration, the fluorescence signal of NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP remained nearly unchanged. These results affirm that the NIP and MIP coatings do not interfere with the fluorescence stability of NCQDs, consistent with findings from prior studies [72,73].

The illegal addition of Sudan III to various complex sample matrices such as food and cosmetics introduces significant challenges to the sensitivity and selectivity of Sudan III detection in real samples. To improve the performance of the fluorimetric sensor, we optimized several parameters by assessing the enhancement of fluorescence intensity in the presence of Sudan III. These parameters include pH solution, buffer compositions, buffer concentration, incubation time, and the amount of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP.

The charges of functional groups on both NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and Sudan III are pH-dependent. Therefore, the optimal pH for the interaction between the composite and Sudan III for fluorescence measurements was investigated across a pH range of 4–9. (Fig. 3 A). The pH of 7.0 emerged as the most favorable value, exhibiting a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity. The surface functional groups of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP primarily consist of primary amine (–NH2) groups, with a pKa range of 10–11. In contrast, Sudan III contains diazo (–N=N–) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups, with pKa values of 2 and 7, respectively. At pH 7, the amine groups of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP become positively charged (–NH3 +), while the hydroxyl groups of Sudan III undergo partial deprotonation, acquiring a negative charge. The diazo groups remain neutral but possess partial negative charge due to the presence of π bonds and lone pairs at nitrogen atoms. However, pH levels below 4 or above 10 may induce ionization of the silica shell and surface defects, leading to reduced photoluminescence and sensitivity of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. In conclusion, pH 7.0 represents the optimum value for enhancing electrostatic interactions between positively charged NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and Sudan III as the acceptor molecule.

The investigation into the optimal buffer type and its concentration was then undertaken to determine the most appropriate conditions for experimental procedures (Fig. 3B). A histidine buffer at a concentration of 0.01 M was identified as the optimized buffer for measurements.

To determine the optimal incubation time for fluorescence detection of Sudan III using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, the fluorescence enhancement signal upon adding Sudan III was examined (Fig. 3C). Generally, the incubation time depends on the adsorption kinetics of Sudan III on NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, ensuring sufficient time for the composite to reach its maximum adsorption capacity. The fluorescence signal exhibited significant improvement from 1 to 15 min of incubation, before remaining relatively stable. Consequently, a 15-minute incubation time was selected for further investigation.

To optimize the amount of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP composite and enhance the method's sensitivity, varying quantities of the composite (ranging from 200 to 500 mg/L) were investigated (Fig. 3D). Increasing the amount of composite resulted in a greater availability of active sites for analytes, thereby leading to a more pronounced enhancement in fluorescence signal. However, beyond a concentration of 500 mg/L, self-absorption of fluorescence may occur, resulting in a slight reduction in sensitivity. Therefore, a concentration of 500 mg/L was selected as the optimized amount of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP composite for subsequent steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regeneration of the Sudan III sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficiency of the sensor for repeated testing, a solution comprising 500 mg/L NCQDs/SiO2@MIP at pH 7.0, containing 450 nM Sudan III, was prepared. After a 15-minute incubation period, the fluorescence signal was recorded. Following this, the mixture underwent two washes with ethanol/acetonitrile (8:2 v/v), each employing 2 mL. Subsequently, centrifugation was carried out to remove template molecules (Sudan III) from the composite sensor. The fluorescence intensity of the material was measured after each wash, revealing that approximately 80 % of the Sudan III could be removed after the first wash (Fig. S5). Following the second wash, nearly 95 % of the Sudan III was eliminated from the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP. Consequently, the material was retested with Sudan III under the initial conditions, demonstrating consistently high recovery efficiency over four consecutive cycles, exceeding 90 % (Fig. S5). However, in the fifth cycle, the recovery efficiency decreased to 85 % compared to the signal obtained with newly synthesized material. Two washes were insufficient to remove 95 % of the Sudan III, suggesting that additional washes or alternative solvents may be required to regenerate the NCQDs-MIP after the fifth use. Nonetheless, the composite sensor exhibits resilience and can be effectively employed for up to four repeated tests with a simple washing step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of Sudan III using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP
ce:section.ce:para: The investigation focused on assessing the influence of varying concentrations of Sudan III on the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs/SiO2@NIP and NCQDs/SiO2@MIP (Fig. 4 ). NCQDs were functionalized with MIPs specifically for the detection of Sudan III. Results indicated a notably higher fluorescence enhancement in CQDs-MIPs compared to CQDs-NIPs upon exposure to Sudan III. This enhancement is attributed to the imprinting of Sudan III on the surface of the CQDs-MIPs.

The concentration of Sudan III showed a direct correlation with the enhancement in fluorescence intensity (F-F0), with regression equations of Y = 1.630 X + 36.957 (R2 = 0.9988) for NCQDs/SiO2@MIP and Y = 0.606 X – 11.141 (R2 = 0.9915) for NCQDs/SiO2@NIP, where X represents the Sudan III concentration (in nM), and Y Y denotes the enhancement in fluorescence intensity (F − F0). The MIP composite sensor exhibited superior linearity, as evidenced by the higher coefficient of determination and a more comprehensive dynamic range of 5–800 nM for NCQDs-MIP compared to 10–600 nM for NCQDs-NIP.

At concentrations above 1500 nM, Sudan III causes a quenching effect on the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, leading to a reduced fluorescence intensity compared to the 1000 nM sample (Fig. S6). To avoid false negatives due to high Sudan III levels, it is essential to choose an appropriate dilution factor that ensures Sudan III levels remain below 800 nM, which is within the linear detection range. The standard addition method can be employed for this purpose. This technique involves adding known quantities of the analyte to the test solution. If these additions result in a decrease in the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP signal, it indicates that the Sudan III concentration in the sample is beyond the calibration curve's working range, requiring a higher dilution factor.

The imprinted factor (IF) of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP was determined by calculating the ratio between the quenching constants observed for CQDs-MIP and CQDs-NIP with Sudan III. These constants correspond to the slope of the calibration curve for each material. In this study, the calculated imprinted factor (IF) was determined to be 2.7, consistent with other reported IF values ranging from 2.0 to 4.4 [74–76].

Our method showed a limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of 1.4 nM (0.49 ppb) and 4.7 nM (1.66 ppb), respectively. In contrast to the assay studied previously (Table 1 ), our method presents a similar LOD alongside the benefits of user-friendliness and the absence of a need for an enrichment step. The determination of these values involved multiplying three times the standard deviation of the blank by the slope of the calibration graph to establish the LOD, and ten times for the LOQ. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD%) for Sudan III concentrations of 50, 200, and 400 nM were 4.87, 3.86, and 4.03 %, respectively, indicating excellent precision (Fig. S7). The recovery values across these three concentrations ranged from 96.7 % to 101.3 %, indicating high accuracy. This highlights the capability of our synthesized NCQDs/SiO2@MIP in effectively quantifying the Sudan III content in real samples.

Selectivity, a crucial analytical factor, denotes the ability of a method to precisely identify a particular analyte within a complex mixture without being influenced by other components. To evaluate the selectivity of our method under ideal circumstances, we recorded the fluorescence signal of NCQDs/SiO2@MIP in the presence of various Sudan dyes (such as Sudan I, II, IV, and 7B) at a concentration of 1.25 µM (Fig. 5 A). Generally, CQDs-MIPs exhibited higher selectivity in responding to Sudan III compared to CQDs-NIPs, likely due to Sudan III's interaction with recognition cavities in the CQDs-MIPs. Both Sudan IV and 7B displayed a fluorescence enhancement effect similar to Sudan III, while the other two dyes exhibited fluorescence quenching. However, the fluorescence change observed in CQDs-MIPs in the presence of other Sudan dyes was significantly smaller compared to the response to Sudan III, even though the concentration of the other Sudan dyes was five times higher than that of Sudan III. This indicates the high specificity of the recognition cavities for Sudan III. Additionally, the selectivity of the Sudan III detection method was evaluated using a standard solution containing 250 nM of Sudan III along with four other Sudan dyes at five times higher concentrations (1.25 µM) (Fig. 5B). The results showed consistent fluorescence intensity recovery, with relative errors of less than 10 %. These results imply that our developed sensor is adept at selectively detecting Sudan III amidst the presence of other Sudan dyes.

Various substances commonly used in cosmetics were introduced to assess the selectivity of the Sudan III determination method, including phenoxyethanol, sodium benzoate, lauric acid, vitamin E, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ag+, amaranth dye, sunset dye, and ponceau 4R, each at a concentration of 25 µM. Phenoxyethanol, sodium benzoate, and lauric acid are typical antimicrobial ingredients in cosmetics, while vitamin E is valued for its antitumorigenic and photoprotective properties. Additionally, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, and Ag+ are utilized as special ingredients for specific purposes such as enhancing spreadability and serving as UV absorbers. Amaranth dye, sunset dye, and ponceau 4R are other commonly used dyes in cosmetics. Alongside assessing the individual effects of these interfering substances on NCQDs/SiO2@MIP, the sensor's selectivity was also tested in the presence of 250 nM Sudan III (100 times lower than the concentration of interferences). The results showed that the fluorescence intensity change of CQDs-MIP remained minimal in the presence of high concentrations of the interfering substances, with most of them causing a fluorescence quenching effect (Fig. 5C). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity enhancement observed upon adding 250 nM Sudan III remained nearly unchanged even at high interference concentrations (250 µM, 1000 times higher than the concentration of Sudan III), with relative errors ranging from −5.48 % to 6.92 %. This indicates that these substances have minimal impact on the accurate detection of Sudan III at the concentrations studied. Overall, the method's high selectivity in determining Sudan III is due to the specific recognition sites for Sudan III within the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lipstick samples analysis and cross-validation by LC-MS/MS
ce:section.ce:para: After successfully synthesizing and optimizing the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP composite for detecting Sudan III, our study applied this composite to analyze Sudan III content in lipstick samples. We obtained lipstick samples from various e-commerce platforms in Vietnam and all of them come from unknown brands, explicitly selecting those with complex compositions containing ingredients like wax, oil, perfume, and pigments. Surprisingly, Sudan III was detected in two out of the five samples, with contents of 257.2 µg/kg and 174.8 µg/kg, respectively (Table 2 ). According to the regulations outlined by the US FDA (21 CFR Part 74 Subpart C—Cosmetics and 21 CFR Part 82 Subparts B, C, and D), Sudan III is not permitted to be present in any lipstick, indicating that these lipstick samples are not safe for use. The remaining three samples did not contain any detectable Sudan III. To evaluate the sensor's capacity for detecting Sudan III in cosmetic samples further, different concentrations of Sudan III standard solutions were added to initially Sudan III-free samples. Each sample treated with Sudan III underwent three analyses, and their average recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were calculated (Table 2). The findings suggest the proposed method holds significant promise in detecting Sudan III traces at the nanomolar (nM) level in lipstick samples accurately. The recoveries of the analyzed samples range from 92 % to 105 %, with RSDs below 10 %.

The lipstick samples were subjected to analysis using liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), which revealed a detection limit (LOD) value of 0.3 ppb. Sudan III was detected in two out of the five samples, consistent with our analysis using NCQDs/SiO2@MIP (Fig.S8). The variation between the results obtained by LC-MS/MS and our method was found to be less than 10 % (Table 2). Comparison between results derived from the NCQDs/SiO2@MIP technique and LC-MS/MS was conducted through statistical analysis of all samples. The results indicate no significant difference between the methodologies, as evidenced by all computed p-values exceeding the 0.05 threshold for a 95 % confidence level (Table S1). This underscores the reliability and effectiveness of our proposed method in precisely determining Sudan III concentration in cosmetic samples. Furthermore, the method we developed demonstrates prompt response times, ease of operation, and sensitivity comparable to that of LC-MS/MS.

This study successfully synthesized and characterized NCQDs/SiO2@MIP composites for detecting Sudan III. Morphological features were extensively analyzed using TEM, SEM, FTIR, XRD, and EDS techniques, confirming successful synthesis and functionalization. Fluorescence spectroscopy revealed significant enhancements in fluorescence intensity upon Sudan III binding. The detection principle elucidated the specific binding of Sudan III onto the MIP layer, leading to fluorescence intensity enhancement. After optimizing analytical parameters, the proposed method demonstrated excellent linearity (R2 of 0.9915) with a broad dynamic range (two orders of magnitude). The method also exhibited a low detection limit (1.4 nM or 0.49 ppb), consistent with previous studies. Our synthesized NCQDs/SiO2@MIP showed robustness across multiple testing cycles. Moreover, the application of the method to analyze Sudan III content in lipstick samples demonstrated its effectiveness and reliability, even in complex matrices. Our method successfully detected Sudan III in two out of five samples, consistent with the results obtained from LC-MS/MS. The consistency observed highlights the potential practical application of our method in quality control and ensuring consumer safety.
